cataloging_subject.label: Magnificencia Ecclesie
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English verse tract conventionally called Magnificencia Ecclesie: thirty-nine eight-line stanzas on the spiritual meanings of the church building, translated from an unidentified Latin source. The record keeps the abstract medieval work distinct from its Latin source, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21, MacCracken's 1909 article and edition, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and modern reproductions. MacCracken's prose introduction and seventeen-line Longfellow quotation are edition paratext, not parts of the medieval poem.

catalog_summary.title: Magnificencia Ecclesie
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet
catalog_summary.date: Circa 1475; attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in 1909

titles.supplied_by: Henry Noble MacCracken's 1909 edition and subsequent scholarship

assertion.notes: A conventional Latin title taken from the manuscript rubric, not evidence of named medieval authorship.

titles.value: Hic sequitur parvus tractatus compendiose in Anglicis translatus de magnificencia ecclesie

assertion.notes: The rubric describes a short tract translated concisely into English on the magnificence of the church.
Retained as catalog attribution history only, distinct from the manuscript's visible Latin rubric. MacCracken rejects Lydgate's authorship, and modern scholarship treats the poem as anonymous.

titles.value: And I wyll pray whyle ye rede eftsone in lyke wyse

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Named only for attribution history. John Stow and later writers associated the poem with Lydgate; MacCracken rejects the attribution, and the work is cataloged here as anonymous.

agents.name: Henry Noble MacCracken
notes: Editor of the Middle English text and author of the modern introduction in the immediate 1909 PMLA article; not the medieval author or translator.

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is secure. The catalog heading and Stow's list preserve Lydgate attribution history without establishing authorship.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator or adapter

assertion.notes: The manuscript rubric explicitly calls the tract translated into English, but the exact Latin source and degree of adaptation remain unresolved.
MacCracken reports that John Stow attributed the poem to Lydgate and rejects the claim on rhyme and style; modern scholarship calls the poem anonymous.

scope.description: Immediate 1909 printed edition of the medieval poem

notes: MacCracken also authored the article's modern introduction and supplied two editorial footnotes; those prose contributions are not medieval work authorship.

date_statements.display: Circa 1475; more broadly the latter half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Summit labels the anonymous poem circa 1475; MacCracken gives the latter half of the fifteenth century. This dates the Middle English work, not every booklet or the present composite codex.

date_statements.display: Attested in fifteenth-century Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21

assertion.notes: The official catalog dates the physical manuscript only to the fifteenth century; narrower dating belongs to attributed scholarship and remains separate from work composition.

date_statements.display: First known publication in MacCracken's PMLA article, December 1909

assertion.notes: Brown cites MacCracken as the poem's edition; no earlier printing was found in the consulted sources, but a complete publication-history audit remains possible.

places.label: England
notes: Used as a broad cultural-origin facet, not as a secure town or region of composition.

assertion.notes: Middle English language and the poem's English ecclesiastical setting support the broad cultural context. MacCracken's speculation about Westminster is not promoted to a composition-place claim.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: MacCracken's modern English introduction and Longfellow quotation belong to the edition layer and are excluded from the work's primary language.

scope.description: Manuscript rubric, opening commandment gloss, and repeated biblical and liturgical quotations within the Middle English poem

form_statements.description: A 312-line poem in thirty-nine eight-line stanzas. The prose-like Latin rubric and brief quotations do not make the whole work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The poem systematically interprets physical church elements as signs of doctrine, clergy, laity, virtue, and spiritual life.

subject_statements.label: Church architecture and its spiritual symbolism
The church as building, institution, and Christian community
Sacraments, commandments, and reverence for the church
Clergy, laity, preaching, and ecclesiastical order
Pride, lechery, covetousness, charity, and spiritual building

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse tract asks why the church is magnified and reads its material fabric as a spiritual and social body. Walls, stones, towers, steeple, weathercock, cross, aisles, windows, pillars, seats, steps, and altar signify the church militant and triumphant, Scripture, clergy, laity, contemplation, charity, and Christ. The poem opens with the first three commandments, urges reverence and disciplined conduct in church, and closes by excluding pride, lechery, and covetousness from the living edifice of Christian people.

The XML contains 329 L elements, but only 312 belong to the medieval poem; the other seventeen form MacCracken's quotation from Longfellow in the modern introduction. The poem itself consists of thirty-nine LG elements, each containing eight direct L children.

extent.description: One complete represented medieval poem: thirty-nine eight-line stanzas and 312 direct verse-line elements. The separate introduction contributes seventeen modern English quotation lines, yielding the XML-wide total of 329 L elements. Eleven numbered printed-page breaks mark pp. 688-698 after the unnumbered opening PB for p. 687; five folio milestones mark ff. 285r, 285v, 286r, 286v, and 287r.

related_works.title: Unidentified Latin treatise on the magnificence of the church
notes: The manuscript rubric and MacCracken establish a Latin-source claim, but no exact text, exemplar, or complete dependency has been identified.

related_works.title: Rationale divinorum officiorum
notes: Laura Varnam argues that the poem draws on William Durandus. This is retained as a scholarly relationship, not a claim that the translator used a particular surviving manuscript or that the complete poem is a direct verse translation of the Rationale.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00067.xml.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY introduction and poem DIV1 elements; HEADNOTE; LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, HI, CHOICE, UNCLEAR, TRAILER, CLOSER, and SIGNED elements
evidence.summary: Confirms the identifier family, complete encoded article boundary, distinction between MacCracken's introduction and the poem, manuscript rubric, incipit, explicit, Middle English and embedded Latin, contents, source page and folio milestones, editorial footnotes, typography, and 2016 repository review.

evidence.citation: XPath and structural inspection of CME00067.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='introduction']; BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; direct LG and L counts; PB and MILESTONE inventories
evidence.summary: Finds 329 XML L elements in all: seventeen in one modern Longfellow quotation and 312 in thirty-nine eight-line medieval stanzas. Also finds twelve PB elements for article pp. 687-698 and five work-folio milestones for ff. 285r-287r.

Companion-lineage evidence for the preferred title, editor, direct Trinity witness, late-fifteenth-century translation claim, anonymous authorship, Stow and Ritson attribution history, rejection of Lydgate, article structure, complete printed poem, and local reconstruction footnotes.
Companion-lineage evidence for article title, author/editor, volume, issue, year, pages, DOI, extract, research-article type, and the publisher's copyright statement.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, R.3.21.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for shelfmark, James number, fifteenth-century date, paper support, 320-folio extent, dimensions, languages, provenance, exact contents entry at f. 285, incipit, explicit, adjacent works, and institutional bibliography.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the complete Trinity R.3.21 IIIF provider object.
evidence.locator: IIIF canvases 573-577 of 686; manuscript ff. 285r-287r; adjacent f. 287v
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the work's exact five-side manuscript range, Latin rubric, incipit, explicit, relation to the following work on f. 287v, complete provider inventory, and visual editorial comparison.

evidence.citation: Carleton Brown, A Register of Middle English Religious & Didactic Verse, part II (Oxford: Bibliographical Society at the University Press, 1920), entry 459.
evidence.summary: Companion-lineage evidence for the incipit, short Latin rubric, thirty-nine eight-line stanzas, Trinity 601 at f. 285a as the listed witness, and MacCracken's PMLA edition.

evidence.citation: Jennifer Summit, Memory's Library: Medieval Books in Early Modern England (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2008), 17.
evidence.summary: Calls Magnificencia ecclesie an anonymous poem of circa 1475 and discusses its closing library passage in the context of late-fifteenth-century book culture.

evidence.citation: Laura Varnam, 'Writing Conference Abstracts,' including the abstract for 'The Parish Church and Its Community in Late-Medieval England' (2018).
evidence.summary: Identifies the poem as an allegorical treatment of church architecture from Trinity R.3.21, dates the manuscript context to 1471-1483, and argues that the text draws on Durandus to teach why readers should honor and magnify the church.
notes: This is a specialist conference abstract rather than a full critical demonstration; its Durandus relationship is therefore qualified and left open for textual verification.

open_questions.question: Which Latin text or texts underlie Magnificencia Ecclesie, and how directly does the Middle English poem depend on Durandus's Rationale divinorum officiorum?
next_steps: Collate the complete poem against Book I of the Rationale and other medieval church-dedication or architectural-allegory compilations.
Separate verbal translation, thematic borrowing, common exegetical tradition, and later adaptation.
Do not identify a physical Latin exemplar without textual and provenance evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous translator's date and place be narrowed independently of the surviving witness, and what evidence exactly generated the Lydgate attribution?
next_steps: Inspect the faint attribution annotation on f. 285r and compare it palaeographically with authenticated Stow annotations across the complete manuscript.
Consult current work on the relevant R.3.21 booklet, its scribe, paper stock, dialect, and circulation.
Reassess MacCracken's rhyme argument and distinguish London booklet production from work composition.

open_questions.question: Does Trinity R.3.21 remain the only known witness, or have post-1920 manuscript discoveries or reidentifications expanded the census?
next_steps: Check the current DIMEV/NIMEV record and recent work-specific bibliography by incipit and rubric.
Search manuscript catalogs for the complete incipit, explicit, and Latin rubric rather than the conventional title alone.
Retain 'unique witness' as a supported discovery tag based on the consulted register, not as proof that no other copy exists.

notes: The catalog summary uses circa 1475 from modern scholarship but preserves the official witness record's broader fifteenth-century date.
MacCracken's unfavorable aesthetic judgments and Westminster speculation are retained only as edition-level evidence, not adopted as neutral work description or secure localization.
Modern English in the article introduction and the current catalog does not make the abstract medieval poem trilingual.
